implementation_summary: Added isAllowedDmSender whitelist gates before every side-effect in both inbound DM subscribers. In directMessageNotifications.ts: added isAllowedSender callback parameter; gated kind-4 handler before seenMessageIds population and all side-effects (AC-SEC-3/4); gated kind-1059 handler before seenRumorIds population and all side-effects (AC-SEC-5); removed bug-documenting comment (AC-OBS-2). In DirectMessageNotificationsWatcher.tsx: added useMarmot() to get groups; passes (peer) => isAllowedDmSender(peer, groups, ownPubkeyHex) as isAllowedSender to the subscription; groups in dep array ensures subscription recreates on membership change. In ContactChat.tsx: added groupsRef (ref-pattern for stable handlers); gated handleHistoricalGiftWrapEvent, handleKind4Event (with isSelf bypass for own-authored echoes on outgoingSub), and handleGiftWrapEvent before all write paths (AC-SEC-6/7); INFO-level drop logging at each gate (AC-OBS-1). All gates run AFTER unwrapAndOpen and shouldIngestRumor, not instead of them (AC-SEC-9/10).

reviews.opus: inline manual review — all 11 ACs verified by code inspection
reviews.codex: sandbox-spawn-failure — reviewer could not access working tree; not a configuration issue

retrospective.harder_than_needed: The isAllowedSender injection point was DirectMessageNotificationsWatcher.tsx (not MarmotContext.tsx as the story guidance suggested). The watcher is a thin component, not the context — the guidance correctly flagged this but required reading the actual call site before the wiring approach was confirmed. Additionally, the kind-4 outgoing echo path (outgoingSub uses authors:[pubkeyHex]) shares handleKind4Event with the inbound path — isAllowedDmSender returns false for own pubkey by design, so a self-bypass check (isSelf) was needed to avoid blocking outgoing message echoes. This edge case was not called out in the spec.
retrospective.surprised_by: The bug-1 regression test in the existing test suite explicitly asserted the old 'any sender' behavior (expecting bell to ring for an unknown peer). Replacing it with new gate-behavior tests was correct per S2's intent, but the existing test comment described it as a regression guard against a real bug — meaning the change inverts a previously load-bearing test. The S4 test story will need to confirm the new expected behavior end-to-end.
